does: This paper introduces PaTaRM, a generative reward model that bridges pairwise and pointwise training paradigms for RLHF. The core contribution is a Preference-Aware Reward (PAR) mechanism that enables pointwise-style training directly from pairwise preference data without requiring expensive absolute ratings. The method also incorporates a Task-Adaptive Rubric system that dynamically generates evaluation criteria specific to each instance, improving interpretability and precision. Experiments on RewardBench and RMBench show 8.7% average improvement across Qwen 3-8B/14B models, with measurable gains in downstream RLHF performance when used to train language models.
connects: This work extends recent research on generative reward models that aim to improve interpretability beyond scalar reward outputs. It addresses a known tension in the reward modeling literature: pairwise training methods suffer from exposure bias and train-test mismatch, while pointwise methods require costly human annotations of absolute quality ratings. The paper builds on prior work in preference learning and RLHF that has explored how to efficiently leverage human feedback, particularly combining insights from contrastive learning and ranking-based approaches with modern generative modeling. It relates to broader efforts in making reward models more transparent and debuggable for language model alignment.
enables: This approach opens several directions for improving RLHF pipelines. By eliminating the need for absolute ratings while maintaining pointwise interpretability, it makes reward model training more practical and cost-effective at scale. The dynamic rubric generation mechanism could inspire further work on instance-aware evaluation criteria and adaptive evaluation standards. The demonstrated improvements in both reward modeling metrics and downstream task performance suggest potential for extending this framework to other domains beyond language modeling, such as code generation or multimodal alignment. Future work could explore how task-adaptive rubrics generalize across diverse domains or how to jointly optimize the rubric generation and reward prediction components.
